subject: Passenger rail wrong fit, wrong century, for Portland
from: Naomi Inman at Cascade Policy Institute <naomi@cascadepolicy.org>
body_text:
https://cascadepolicy.org/ 

**Transportation policy should reflect how people travel today—**

**not how cities worked 125 years ago**

https://cascadepolicy.org/transportation/passenger-rail-wrong-fit-for-portland-metro-study-shows/ 

**Passenger Rail Wrong Fit for Portland,**

**Metro Study Shows**

Dear Benjamin,

This week, Cascade Policy Institute president John Charles sent a letter to the members of Oregon’s House and Senate about Metro’s Regional Rail Futures Study https://ti.org/pdfs/MetroRegionalRailFuturesStudy.pdf, prepared in response to a 2024 legislative mandate in Senate Bill 5701 https://olis.oregonlegislature.gov/liz/2024R1/Downloads/MeasureDocument/SB5701/Enrolled, Section 503.

**SECTION 503. In addition to and not in lieu of any other appropriation, there is appropriated to the Department of Transportation, for the biennium ending June 30, 2025, out of the General Fund, the amount of $500,000, for distribution to the metropolitan service district organized under ORS chapter 268 to study the use of existing heavy freight rail assets in the Portland metropolitan area for passenger rail alternatives to existing transportation modes. **

John attached a published report https://cascadepolicy.org/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/Review-of-Metros-Regional-Passenger-Rail-Futures-Study-FINAL.pdf by Cascade adjunct scholar, Randal O’Toole, who reviewed the study which identifies major problems with regional passenger rail in the Portland area, including high operating costs, the need for major infrastructure improvements, congestion on existing freight lines, limited high ridership along many corridors, and weak ridership prospects in some corridors.

Yet despite those findings, Metro declined to rule out regional rail and instead continued to encourage large-scale rail-oriented investments and additional land-use changes near freight corridors.

That approach reflects a larger planning problem. Portland-area transit has long been organized around downtown as the central hub, even though downtown now represents a small share of regional jobs compared with a century ago. In today’s metropolitan economy, over 90 percent are dispersed across the region, not centered on downtown Portland. A transit strategy built around fixed rail and a single dominant hub does not match reality.

A more practical approach would focus on improving bus service and designing a network that better serves the region as it exists today, including suburban employment centers and other major destinations outside downtown. Buses are more flexible, less expensive, and better suited to adapting to changing travel patterns than rail. 

John’s letter https://cascadepolicy.org/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/Letter-to-Legislators-on-Regl-Rail-Futures-PDF.pdf and supporting report https://cascadepolicy.org/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/Review-of-Metros-Regional-Passenger-Rail-Futures-Study-FINAL.pdf asks legislators to give careful consideration about whether state support for regional passenger rail is justified in light of Metro’s own findings.

READ JOHN CHARLES LETTER TO OREGON LEGISLATORS ON RAIL STUDY  https://cascadepolicy.org/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/Letter-to-Legislators-on-Regl-Rail-Futures-PDF.pdf

READ RANDAL O’TOOLE’S REPORT, “A REVIEW OF METRO’S REGIONAL PASSENGER RAIL FUTURES STUDY” https://cascadepolicy.org/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/Review-of-Metros-Regional-Passenger-Rail-Futures-Study-FINAL.pdf

Listen to Quick Point https://open.spotify.com/show/76vcnobydxd2VBZ2TD2Ine?si=7b32f071861b45f6

Read More https://cascadepolicy.org/transportation/passenger-rail-wrong-fit-for-portland-metro-study-shows/

Subscribe to Our Newsletter https://cascadepolicy.org/news-letter/

https://cascadepolicy.org/transportation/wes-commuter-rail-should-be-shut-down-not-expanded/ 

WES Commuter Rail Should be Shut Down, Not Expanded https://cascadepolicy.org/transportation/wes-commuter-rail-should-be-shut-down-not-expanded/

While it sounds nice to run a train route from Portland to Eugene via the Westside Express Service (WES), the numbers don't add up. Ridership has plummeted and it doesn't make sense to pour hundreds of millions into a "hunch" or a hope for new riders. No reasonable case can be made for increasing WES service, while cases can be made for its demise.

Read More https://cascadepolicy.org/transportation/wes-commuter-rail-should-be-shut-down-not-expanded/

https://cascadepolicy.org/transportation/trimet-retreat-should-focus-on-crisis-not-expansion/ 

TriMet Retreat Should Focus on Crisis, Not Expansion https://cascadepolicy.org/transportation/trimet-retreat-should-focus-on-crisis-not-expansion/

In November, Cascade's John Charles attended TriMet's Board Retreat and suggested that TriMet take a closer look at their financial scorecard rather that dreaming about expanding lightrail into Washington state. As TriMet is facing $850 million in operating losses in 2024, how can they think about expansion before addressing at the bottom-line? 

Read More https://cascadepolicy.org/transportation/trimet-retreat-should-focus-on-crisis-not-expansion/

**Promoting Freedom & Opportunity for 35 Years!**

**A look back over Cascade's influence in Oregon Policy** 

**2016**

In the early 2010s, the Endangered Species Act litigation reduced logging in the Elliot State Forest, which therefore stopped generating net revenue for the Common School Fund.  Cascade was one of the key free-market voices pushing the Board to treat the forest as a financial asset and consider selling it.  

While the Board changed their direction, Cascade's work opened the door to viewing forests as fiduciary assets and means to benefit state programs.

**Cascade in Action**

**3/20/2026 -** 

**The San Diego Union-Tribune** published an Op-Ed https://www.sandiegouniontribune.com/2026/03/20/opinion-stop-digging-the-public-transit-money-pit/ by Adam Summers citing Cascade adjunct scholar 

**Randal O’Toole**, “Opinion: Increasing transit subsidies as ridership declines is crazy.”

**3/19/2026 -** 

**Seattle Red 770AM** published an Op-Ed https://seattlered.com/seattle-red/opinion/i5-bridge-replacement-cost/4117310 by Bob Pishue citing 

**Cascade Policy Institute**, “Bob Pishue: The ballooning cost of the I-5 bridge between Oregon and Washington is unjustified.”

**3/19/2026 -** 

**The News Tribune** published an Op-Ed https://www.thenewstribune.com/opinion/article315099447.html by Bob Pishue citing 

**Cascade Policy Institute**, “Light Rail on I-5 bridge will cost Washington taxpayers dearly | Opinion.”

**3/18/2026 -** 

**Clark County Today** published an Op-Ed https://www.clarkcountytoday.com/opinion/opinion-the-ballooning-cost-of-the-i-5-bridge-between-oregon-and-washington-is-unjustified/ by the Mountain States Policy Center citing 

**Cascade Policy Institute**, “Op-Ed: The ballooning cost of the I-5 bridge is unjustified.”

**3/18/2026  -** 

**The Center Square** published an Op-Ed by the Mountain States Policy Center citing 

**Cascade Policy Institute**, “Op-Ed: The ballooning cost of the I-5 bridge is unjustified.”

**3/17/2026 -** 

**The New Era** published an Op-Ed https://www.sweethomenews.com/opinion-time-to-end-the-death-tax/ by 

**John A. Charles, Jr.** “Opinion: Time to end the death tax.”

**3/17/2026 -**  

**The Antiplanner** and Cascade adjunct scholar 

**Randal O’Toole** cited https://ti.org/antiplanner/?m=20260317 

**Cascade Policy Institute**, “One More Excuse to Densify Portland”

**3/17/2026 -** 

**Oregon Catalyst** published a post https://oregoncatalyst.com/95403-cascade-testimony-metro-spending-50m-40-years-turning-superfund-site-park.html by 

**Naomi Inman** and 

**John Charles**, “Cascade Testimony: Metro Spending $50M, 40 Years, Turning Superfund Site to a Park.”

**3/13/2026 -**  

**Cascade Policy Institute** provided supporting documents to the U.S. Dept of Education Office for Civil Rights.

**Policy Watch**

**Articles of note on issues you care about**

**Economy**

**2/26/26 -**

**After Party with Emily Jashinsky** | How Portland Destroyed Itself from Within as Progressive Experiment Backfires, with Mark Hemingway https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IekXEthI_9o (Video 18:50)

**Schools**

**3/13/2026 -** 

**The Oregonian** | Which Portland schools could close in 2027? PPS board launches districtwide review https://www.oregonlive.com/education/2026/03/which-portland-schools-could-close-in-2027-pps-board-launches-districtwide-review.html?utm_source=custom-alerts&utm_medium=email

**3/4/2026 -** 

**Education Week** | They Said No to the Federal School Choice Program. Now, 3 Dems Are Reconsidering https://www.edweek.org/policy-politics/they-said-no-to-the-federal-school-choice-program-now-3-dems-are-reconsidering/2026/03 : Governors have to decide whether their states will participate

**Taxes**

**3/19/2026 -** 

**Unleash Prosperity** | Living in a Blue State is Harmful to Your Wealth https://committeetounleashprosperity.com/hotlines/living-in-a-blue-state-is-harmful-to-your-wealth/

**3/11/2026 -** 

**The Oregonian** | Oregon PERS retirees 2025: Database updated with 6,400 new beneficiaries - oregonlive.com https://www.oregonlive.com/data/2026/03/oregons-most-generous-pers-formula-is-going-extinct-but-its-still-minting-six-figure-retirees.html?e=81f47344afddbaa341d9779cc9bcc5b2&lctg=60f9bc2f03a9932bc96add40&utm_source=Sailthru&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=Newsletter_portland_oregon_politics%202026-03-11&utm_term=Newsletter_portland_oregon_politics

**Transit & Rail**

**9/22/2020 -**

**Reason Foundation** | Reimagining Transportation Policy During and After COVID-19 https://reason.org/policy-brief/reimagining-transportation-policy-during-and-after-covid-19/

**5/23/2017 -**

**Cato Institute** | 10 Reasons to Stop Subsidizing Urban Transit https://www.cato.org/blog/10-reasons-stop-subsidizing-transit

**Unions**

**3/19/26 -** 

**Oregon Roundup on Substack** |  SEIU withholds dues info from director - by Jeff Eager https://oregonroundup.substack.com/p/seiu-withholds-dues-info-from-director

**Policy Picnic with Cascade - April 15**
Auditing the Interstate Bridge Project
Rep. John Ley (WA Dist. 18)

**Auditing the Interstate Bridge Project**

**with Rep. John Ley (Vancouver, WA)**

**Wednesday, April 15, Noon to 1 p.m.**

Rep. John Ley has introduced legislation to bring greater transparency, accountability, and fiscal responsibility to the Interstate 5 Bridge Replacement Project, one of the largest and most expensive transportation projects in the history of the Pacific Northwest.

Read about John Ley's legislation at Rep. John Ley’s new bill calls for an independent audit of Interstate 5 Bridge Replacement Project – ClarkCountyToday.com https://www.clarkcountytoday.com/news/rep-john-leys-new-bill-calls-for-an-independent-audit-of-interstate-5-bridge-replacement-project/.

Reserve Your Seat https://www.eventbrite.com/e/policy-picnic-with-cascade-tickets-1985614824391?aff=oddtdtcreator&keep_tld=true

**Foundation for Economic Education**
March 28 Event in Tacoma

Cooperation without Coercion:
The Beauty of Voluntary Society

Join 

**FEE President Diogo Costa** and 

**FEE President Emeritus Lawrence W. Reed** for breakfast and a half-day conversation on voluntary cooperation, free markets, and how to discuss economic ideas thoughtfully in today’s political climate.

* Saturday, March 28

* Silver Cloud Hotel Tacoma at Point Ruston Waterfront

* Doors open at 8:00 a.m.

Register Now https://fee.org/cooperation-without-coercion/

**Opportunities at Cascade**

Cascade Policy Institute is Hiring – Development Assistant https://cascadepolicy.org/news/cascade-policy-institute-is-hiring-development-assistant/

Cascade Policy Institute is seeking a part-time Development Assistant to work with our team on development, administrative, and donor relations work. This in-office position is approximately 12-16 hours per week. We're a friendly group with a passion for free-market economics and a better Oregon. Please read our job description and apply today.

Cascade Policy Institute's 2026 Internship Program is Open  https://cascadepolicy.org/internship/

Do you know a college student who wants to become a more effective advocate for free markets and limited government in the Pacific Northwest? Cascade's 2026 summer internship program is now open for applications. Cascade interns spend the summer as junior research analysts, while enjoying beautiful Portland, Oregon, and building their resumes. 

Since 1991, Cascade Policy Institute has worked to develop and advance ideas in public policy that 

**foster individual liberty, personal responsibility, and economic opportunity for all Oregonians.** We are your voice in 2026 to expand opportunity and freedom in Oregon. Thank you for supporting https://soshily.cascadepolicy.org/lt.php?x=3TxtmrUFUqPUT55qA3P3U.hz~3JTjdEfvuwzZ5HKInKc55Gszdu5guhy1HzTiNVfx1O3X5MWKXif950KyN~JUeZ this important mission.

Transportation policy should reflect how people travel today—

not how cities worked 125 years ago

Passenger Rail Wrong Fit for Portland,

Metro Study Shows

Dear Benjamin,


This week, Cascade Policy Institute president John Charles sent a letter to the members of Oregon’s House and Senate about Metro’s Regional Rail Futures Study, prepared in response to a 2024 legislative mandate in Senate Bill 5701, Section 503.


SECTION 503. In addition to and not in lieu of any other appropriation, there is appropriated to the Department of Transportation, for the biennium ending June 30, 2025, out of the General Fund, the amount of $500,000, for distribution to the metropolitan service district organized under ORS chapter 268 to study the use of existing heavy freight rail assets in the Portland metropolitan area for passenger rail alternatives to existing transportation modes.

Portland-area transit has long been organized around downtown as the central hub, even though downtown now represents a small share of regional jobs compared with a century ago. A more practical approach would focus on improving bus service and designing a network that better serves the region as it exists today, including suburban employment centers and other major destinations outside downtown. John’s letter and supporting report asks legislators to give careful consideration about whether state support for regional passenger rail is justified in light of Metro’s own findings.

Foundation for Economic Education
March 28 Event in Tacoma

Cooperation without Coercion:
The Beauty of Voluntary Society


Join FEE President Diogo Costa and FEE President Emeritus Lawrence W. Reed for breakfast and a half-day conversation on voluntary cooperation, free markets, and how to discuss economic ideas thoughtfully in today’s political climate.

  • Saturday, March 28
  • Silver Cloud Hotel Tacoma at Point Ruston Waterfront
  • Doors open at 8:00 a.m.
Register Now

Opportunities at Cascade

Cascade Policy Institute is Hiring – Development Assistant

Cascade Policy Institute is seeking a part-time Development Assistant to work with our team on development, administrative, and donor relations work. This in-office position is approximately 12-16 hours per week. We're a friendly group with a passion for free-market economics and a better Oregon. Please read our job description and apply today.

Cascade Policy Institute's 2026 Internship Program is Open 

Do you know a college student who wants to become a more effective advocate for free markets and limited government in the Pacific Northwest? Cascade's 2026 summer internship program is now open for applications. Cascade interns spend the summer as junior research analysts, while enjoying beautiful Portland, Oregon, and building their resumes. 

Since 1991, Cascade Policy Institute has worked to develop and advance ideas in public policy that foster individual liberty, personal responsibility, and economic opportunity for all Oregonians. We are your voice in 2026 to expand opportunity and freedom in Oregon. Thank you for supporting this important mission.

Have you connected with Cascade Policy Institute on Facebook, Instagram and X? Please join us today @CascadePolicy and share Cascade with your friends.

We have updated our Privacy Policy and Terms and Conditions. Please read both of these documents as the changes affect your legal rights.
